<!-- Auto-generated by BioNexus PNS+spinal-cord research workflow. Cited, adversarially reviewed. -->

# Peripheral nerves & spinal cord — how they should look (BioNexus)

This reference defines the correct anatomical form, regional segmentation, and colour treatment for two neural layers in the BioNexus 3D viewer: the **peripheral nervous system (PNS)** and the **spinal cord**. It exists to give both content authors and reviewers a single, citable standard for "what right looks like" — so that nerves are grouped by real anatomical region (not by an arbitrary left/right split) and rendered in the classic atlas colour, and so that the spinal cord is modelled as a true cord that terminates near L1, carries a genuine lumbar region, and continues below as the cauda equina. The two descriptive sections below are followed by a concrete **acceptance checklist** that turns these facts into pass/fail criteria for QA.

---

## Peripheral nervous system — organization for a 3D viewer

The peripheral nervous system (PNS) is everything outside the brain and spinal cord. For a 3D anatomy viewer it is most useful to think of the PNS as a **branching tree with two roots** — the cranial nerves leaving the brain, and the spinal nerves leaving the spinal cord — that then reorganizes into a handful of **plexuses** feeding discrete body regions. This section defines that tree, the named nerves a viewer should expose, the region buckets to segment it into, and the color convention for rendering it.

### 1. The two roots of the tree: cranial vs. spinal nerves

**Cranial nerves — 12 pairs (CN I–XII).** These emerge directly from the brain and brainstem and exit through foramina/fissures of the skull; they are numbered rostral-to-caudal by their exit point. Two arise from the **forebrain** and the other ten from the **brainstem** — a distinction worth encoding as viewer metadata:

| CN | Name | Origin |
|----|------|--------|
| I | Olfactory | Forebrain (cerebrum / olfactory bulb) |
| II | Optic | Forebrain (diencephalon) |
| III | Oculomotor | Midbrain |
| IV | Trochlear | Midbrain (only nerve exiting dorsally) |
| V | Trigeminal | Pons |
| VI | Abducens | Pons (pontomedullary junction) |
| VII | Facial | Pons (pontomedullary junction) |
| VIII | Vestibulocochlear | Pons (pontomedullary junction) |
| IX | Glossopharyngeal | Medulla oblongata |
| X | Vagus | Medulla oblongata |
| XI | Accessory | Upper cervical spinal cord (mainly); small/variable cranial root from the medulla |
| XII | Hypoglossal | Medulla oblongata |

Key facts to surface: **olfactory (CN I) and optic (CN II) arise from the forebrain**, while CN III–XII arise from the brainstem (midbrain → III, IV; pons → V–VIII; medulla → IX–XII — with the caveat that the **accessory nerve (XI) arises mainly from the upper cervical spinal cord (≈C1–C5)**, its cranial/medullary root being small and inconstant). Cranial nerves supply the head and neck; the **vagus (X)** is the exception, extending down to innervate thoracic and abdominal viscera ([Kenhub — The 12 cranial nerves](https://www.kenhub.com/en/library/anatomy/the-12-cranial-nerves); [TeachMeAnatomy — Cranial nerves summary](https://teachmeanatomy.info/head/cranial-nerves/summary/); [Wikipedia — Cranial nerves](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cranial_nerves)).

**Spinal nerves — 31 pairs.** Each exits the vertebral column through an intervertebral foramen and is numbered superior-to-inferior:

- **8 cervical** (C1–C8)
- **12 thoracic** (T1–T12)
- **5 lumbar** (L1–L5)
- **5 sacral** (S1–S5)
- **1 coccygeal** (Co1)

Every spinal nerve is *mixed* (motor + sensory) and quickly splits into a **posterior (dorsal) ramus** supplying the back and a larger **anterior (ventral) ramus** supplying the limbs and the front/side of the trunk. It is the anterior rami that interweave to form the plexuses below ([Kenhub — Spinal nerves](https://www.kenhub.com/en/library/anatomy/spinal-nerves); [Wikipedia — Spinal nerve](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spinal_nerve)).

### 2. The four major plexuses (plus the un-plexed thoracic nerves)

A **plexus** is a network where anterior rami exchange fibers, so a single peripheral nerve carries fibers from several spinal levels. Four major plexuses matter for a whole-body viewer; the thoracic region deliberately has **no plexus**.

**Cervical plexus (C1–C4).** Region: neck, and — critically — the diaphragm. Its headline branch is the **phrenic nerve (C3, C4, C5 → "C3, 4, 5 keep the diaphragm alive")**, the sole motor supply to the diaphragm (its C5 fibres frequently reach it via an accessory phrenic nerve of brachial-plexus level). Cutaneous branches (lesser occipital, great auricular, transverse cervical, supraclavicular) cover neck skin; the ansa cervicalis drives the strap muscles ([Kenhub — Cervical plexus](https://www.kenhub.com/en/library/anatomy/cervical-plexus); [TeachMeAnatomy — Cervical plexus](https://teachmeanatomy.info/neck/nerves/cervical-plexus/); [Kenhub — Phrenic nerve](https://www.kenhub.com/en/library/anatomy/phrenic-nerve)).

**Brachial plexus (C5–T1) → upper limb.** The most complex plexus (roots → trunks → divisions → cords → branches). Expose its **five terminal branches**:

- **Musculocutaneous** — anterior arm flexors (biceps, brachialis, coracobrachialis)
- **Axillary** — deltoid, teres minor; shoulder
- **Radial** — posterior arm/forearm extensors; the "great extensor nerve"
- **Median** — most anterior forearm flexors, thenar muscles ("labourer's nerve")
- **Ulnar** — intrinsic hand muscles, medial hand ("musician's nerve")

([Kenhub — Brachial plexus](https://www.kenhub.com/en/library/anatomy/brachial-plexus); [TeachMeAnatomy — Brachial plexus](https://teachmeanatomy.info/upper-limb/nerves/brachial-plexus/)).

**Thoracic / intercostal nerves (T1–T11; T12 = subcostal) — NO plexus.** The anterior rami of the thoracic spinal nerves stay **segmental**, running individually in the intercostal spaces to supply the muscles and skin of the **thoracic and upper abdominal wall** (the trunk wall). This banded, one-nerve-per-level pattern is why the thorax is the only region without a plexus, and it makes the thoracic wall the easiest region to segment cleanly in a viewer ([Wikipedia — Intercostal nerves](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Intercostal_nerves); [Kenhub — Spinal nerves](https://www.kenhub.com/en/library/anatomy/spinal-nerves)).

**Lumbar plexus (L1–L4) → lower trunk & anterior/medial thigh.** Headline branches:

- **Femoral nerve** — anterior thigh (quadriceps, hip flexion, knee extension); continues as the saphenous nerve to the medial leg
- **Obturator nerve** — medial thigh adductors

(plus iliohypogastric, ilioinguinal, genitofemoral, and lateral femoral cutaneous nerves) ([Kenhub — Lower limb arteries and nerves](https://www.kenhub.com/en/library/anatomy/lower-extremities-arteries-and-nerves); [Wikipedia — Lumbar plexus](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lumbar_plexus)).

**Sacral plexus (L4–S4) → posterior thigh, leg, foot, pelvis.** Headline branches:

- **Sciatic nerve (L4–S3)** — the **largest and thickest nerve in the body**; supplies the hamstrings, then divides (usually at the superior angle of the popliteal fossa) into its two terminal parts:
  - **Tibial nerve** → posterior leg and sole of the foot
  - **Common fibular (common peroneal) nerve** → lateral leg and dorsum of the foot
- **Pudendal nerve (S2–S4)** — perineum, external genitalia, and sphincters
- (plus superior/inferior gluteal nerves to the gluteal muscles)

The lumbar and sacral plexuses are linked by the **lumbosacral trunk (L4–L5)** and are frequently treated together as the **lumbosacral plexus** ([Kenhub — Sacral plexus](https://www.kenhub.com/en/library/anatomy/sacral-plexus); [Kenhub — Sciatic nerve](https://www.kenhub.com/en/library/anatomy/the-sciatic-nerve); [TeachMeAnatomy — Sacral plexus](https://teachmeanatomy.info/lower-limb/nerves/sacral-plexus/)).

### 3. Region-to-nerve mapping for viewer segmentation

Collapse the tree into **four selectable region buckets**. This mapping is the recommended segmentation for a peripheral-nerve layer:

| Viewer region | Source levels / plexus | Major named nerves to expose | Territory served |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Head / cranial** | Cranial nerves I–XII (brain & brainstem) | Olfactory (I) … Hypoglossal (XII); e.g. optic (II), oculomotor (III), trigeminal (V), facial (VII), vagus (X) | Head & neck; vagus reaches thoracic/abdominal viscera |
| **Neck + upper limb** | Cervical plexus (C1–C4) + Brachial plexus (C5–T1) | **Phrenic** (neck→diaphragm); **musculocutaneous, axillary, radial, median, ulnar** | Neck, diaphragm, shoulder, arm, forearm, hand |
| **Thorax / trunk wall** | Thoracic anterior rami (T1–T11 intercostal; T12 subcostal) — *no plexus, segmental* | Intercostal nerves 1–11, subcostal nerve | Thoracic & upper abdominal wall (muscles + skin) |
| **Lower trunk + lower limb** | Lumbosacral plexus: Lumbar (L1–L4) + Sacral (L4–S4) | **Femoral, obturator** (lumbar); **sciatic → tibial + common fibular**, **pudendal**, gluteals (sacral) | Lower abdomen, pelvis/perineum, thigh, leg, foot |

Practical notes for segmentation: (a) the **thorax bucket is the clean, banded, one-nerve-per-segment region** because it has no plexus; (b) the **vagus (X)** should be allowed to cross out of the head bucket into the thorax/abdomen, as it is the one cranial nerve with a long thoracoabdominal course; (c) **T1 partly feeds the brachial plexus** and **T12/L4 straddle the lumbar boundary**, so a few root fibers legitimately span two buckets — model boundary nerves as shared rather than duplicated.

### 4. Color and appearance conventions

Follow the long-standing anatomical-atlas convention for the "vessels and nerves" trio so users read structures at a glance:

- **Nerves → pale yellow / ivory** (the classic Netter/Gray's rendering for peripheral nerve trunks)
- Arteries → red
- Veins → blue

Rendering peripheral nerves in a soft, desaturated **yellow-ivory** distinguishes them from the red arterial and blue venous layers and matches learners' expectations from printed atlases ([Kenhub — Major arteries, veins and nerves of the body](https://www.kenhub.com/en/library/anatomy/major-arteries-veins-and-nerves-of-the-body)). For a viewer, keep nerve yellow slightly muted/matte (rather than pure saturated yellow) so overlapping nerve, artery, and vein layers stay legible, and consider a brighter yellow highlight on hover/selection. Crucially, the **same pale-yellow material applies to every peripheral-nerve region and to both sides of the body** — regional segmentation is expressed through the *selectable region grouping and metadata*, never by painting different regions (or the left vs. right side) different colours.

---

## Spinal cord — external anatomy and regional segmentation

### Overall form

The spinal cord is the elongated, roughly cylindrical part of the central nervous system that occupies the upper two-thirds of the vertebral canal. In the adult it measures approximately **42–45 cm** in length and is continuous superiorly with the **medulla oblongata** of the brainstem at the level of the **foramen magnum**, where the two are conventionally divided at the emergence of the first pair of cervical (C1) nerve roots [1][2]. Inferiorly the cord does **not** run the full length of the vertebral canal; instead it tapers to a cone-shaped termination, the **conus medullaris**, which in the adult lies at approximately the **L1–L2 vertebral level** (commonly described as the lower border of L1 or the L1/L2 disc) [1][3]. This mismatch arises because the vertebral column outgrows the spinal cord during development, so the cord "ascends" relative to the bony canal — an important reason why spinal cord segments do not lie opposite their same-numbered vertebrae in the lower spine [1][3].

The cord is not uniformly thick along its length. Its transverse diameter is greater than its anteroposterior diameter, and it bears two fusiform **enlargements** where the limb-innervating nerve roots attach (see Regions, below) [2]. Externally it is marked by longitudinal grooves — the deep **anterior median fissure** and the shallower **posterior median sulcus** — that partially divide it into symmetrical halves [2].

### The four functional regions and 31 segments

Although the cord is anatomically continuous and lacks visible internal partitions, it is functionally divided into regions defined by the **spinal nerves** that arise from it. There are **31 pairs of spinal nerves** and therefore **31 segments**, grouped as **8 cervical, 12 thoracic, 5 lumbar, 5 sacral, and 1 coccygeal** [1][4]. The four principal functional regions requested here are, from superior to inferior:

- **Cervical region — 8 segments (C1–C8).** The uppermost region. It contains the **cervical enlargement**, which spans roughly spinal segments **C4–T1** (C5–T1 in some sources) and gives rise to the roots of the **brachial plexus** supplying the **upper limbs**; this is why the cord is visibly broader here [2][4]. Note that there are 8 cervical spinal segments but only 7 cervical vertebrae.
- **Thoracic region — 12 segments (T1–T12).** The **long middle** portion of the cord. It is comparatively **thinner and more uniform** in caliber because it supplies the trunk wall rather than a limb, and it occupies the greatest vertical extent of the cord [2][4].
- **Lumbar region — 5 segments (L1–L5).**
- **Sacral region — 5 segments (S1–S5).**

The lumbar and sacral segments together form the **lumbosacral (lumbar) enlargement**, which lies at approximately the **T11–L1 vertebral level** and provides the roots of the **lumbar and sacral plexuses** for the **lower limbs** [2][4]. Because these segments are packed into a short length of cord just above the conus, the lumbar and sacral regions are **spatially compressed** near the bottom of the cord — occupying far less vertical length than their nerve-supply territory in the body would suggest.

**Spatial proportions for segmentation.** When segmenting the cord along its length, the correct top-to-bottom layout is: a moderately long **cervical** region at the top (widened by the cervical enlargement), a **long, slender thoracic** region through the middle, and then a **short, crowded lower zone** where the **lumbar + sacral** segments and the terminal **conus medullaris** are stacked together within roughly the last few centimetres. In vertebral terms, cervical segments lie near their corresponding vertebrae at the top, thoracic segments increasingly sit above their numbered vertebrae, and the lumbar and sacral cord segments are collapsed into the lower thoracic-to-L1 vertebral levels [1][3].

### Conus medullaris, cauda equina, and filum terminale

The cord ends as the tapered **conus medullaris** at the L1–L2 vertebral level [1][3]. Because the sacral and lumbar cord segments sit well above their intervertebral exit foramina, their long nerve roots must descend nearly vertically within the canal before exiting. This descending bundle of **lumbar, sacral, and coccygeal nerve roots** below the conus is the **cauda equina** ("horse's tail"), so named for its brush-like appearance [1][3]. From the tip of the conus a slender strand of **pia mater**, the **filum terminale**, continues downward; its upper part (filum terminale internum) runs within the dural sac, and its lower part (filum terminale externum) anchors the cord to the dorsum of the **coccyx**, providing longitudinal stabilization [1][3]. The presence of the cauda equina (rather than the cord itself) in the lower lumbar canal is what makes **lumbar puncture** below L2 relatively safe [1][3].

### Meningeal coverings

Like the brain, the spinal cord is enclosed by three connective-tissue membranes, the **meninges**, arranged from outermost to innermost [1][5]:

- **Dura mater** — the tough, fibrous outer layer forming the dural (thecal) sac; separated from the vertebral periosteum by the fat- and vein-containing **epidural space** [1][5].
- **Arachnoid mater** — the thin, avascular intermediate layer lying against the dura, deep to which is the **subarachnoid space** filled with **cerebrospinal fluid (CSF)** [1][5].
- **Pia mater** — the delicate, vascular innermost layer that adheres directly to the cord surface, extends laterally as the tooth-like **denticulate ligaments** that suspend the cord within the dural sac, and continues inferiorly as the filum terminale [1][5].

The dural sac and subarachnoid (CSF) space extend below the conus down to about the **S2 vertebral level**, forming the **lumbar cistern** in which the cauda equina floats [1][3].

### Colour and surface appearance

In the fresh or preserved state the spinal cord has a soft, glistening surface and appears **whitish to cream-coloured, often with a pinkish or greyish tinge** [2][6]. The pale, whitish external colour reflects the myelinated **white matter** that forms the outer mantle of the cord (in contrast to the internal grey matter), while the pinkish cast derives from its vascular pia-covered surface [2][6].

---

## Visual & segmentation acceptance checklist

Each item below is a concrete pass/fail check. A build is "correct" only when every applicable box can be ticked.

### (a) Peripheral nerves — regional segmentation & uniform pale yellow

- [ ] **Four anatomical region buckets exist** and are the *only* way nerves are grouped: **(1) Head / cranial**, **(2) Neck + upper limb (cervical + brachial / upper-limb)**, **(3) Thorax / intercostal (trunk wall)**, **(4) Lower trunk + lower limb (lumbosacral)**. Selecting a region isolates that bucket.
- [ ] **Grouping is by anatomical region, NOT by side.** There is **no left/right split** used as the segmentation scheme — i.e. the model must NOT be divided into merely "left nerves" and "right nerves". Bilateral nerves (e.g. left and right median) both belong to the *same* region bucket.
- [ ] **A single, uniform nerve colour is used everywhere.** Every peripheral-nerve region and both body sides share **one pale-yellow / ivory material**. The build FAILS if two regions render in two different colours, or if left and right sides render in different colours.
- [ ] **The nerve colour is pale/desaturated yellow (ivory)** — muted/matte, not pure saturated primary yellow — and is visibly distinct from arteries (red) and veins (blue) in the same scene.
- [ ] **Each region exposes its expected named nerves** (spot-check): cranial region includes CN I–XII (optic, trigeminal, facial, vagus, etc.); neck+upper-limb includes **phrenic** plus **musculocutaneous, axillary, radial, median, ulnar**; thorax includes **intercostal nerves 1–11 + subcostal**; lower limb includes **femoral, obturator, sciatic → tibial + common fibular, pudendal**.
- [ ] **The thoracic bucket reads as banded / segmental** (one nerve per intercostal level, no plexus), confirming the regions reflect real anatomy rather than an arbitrary partition.
- [ ] **Boundary nerves are shared, not duplicated or miscoloured** — the **vagus (X)** may extend from the head bucket into thorax/abdomen, and root fibres at **T1** (into brachial plexus) and **T12/L4** may span two buckets, without breaking the single-colour rule.
- [ ] **Hover/selection highlight** (if present) brightens the yellow or outlines the structure; it does not introduce a permanent second base colour for a region.

### (b) Spinal cord — cord form, regional segmentation, conus & cauda equina

- [ ] **Rendered as a single continuous cord** (elongated, roughly cylindrical), continuous superiorly with the **medulla oblongata** at the foramen magnum — not as a stack of disconnected blocks.
- [ ] **The cord terminates high, around L1** — it tapers to a cone (**conus medullaris**) at roughly the **L1–L2 vertebral level** and does **NOT** run the full length of the vertebral canal.
- [ ] **The cord is segmentable into four regions: cervical, thoracic, lumbar, sacral** (top-to-bottom), selectable independently.
- [ ] **A LUMBAR region actually exists.** The build FAILS if the lumbar region is missing, empty, or collapsed into thoracic/sacral. There must be a distinct, selectable lumbar segment group (L1–L5).
- [ ] **Regional proportions are anatomically plausible:** a moderately long **cervical** region at top (widened by the **cervical enlargement**), a **long slender thoracic** region through the middle, and a **short, crowded lower zone** where **lumbar + sacral** segments (the **lumbosacral enlargement**) are compressed just above the conus.
- [ ] **Cauda equina is present BELOW the conus** — a brush-like descending bundle of lumbar, sacral, and coccygeal nerve roots continuing down from the conus tip. The cord proper must end at ~L1 with the cauda equina (not more cord) filling the lower canal.
- [ ] **Filum terminale** (thin pial strand from the conus tip toward the coccyx) is present or at least not contradicted (optional but preferred).
- [ ] **Two enlargements are visible** where the cord is broader: **cervical** (≈C4–T1, feeds the upper limbs via the brachial plexus) and **lumbosacral** (≈T11–L1 vertebral level, feeds the lower limbs).
- [ ] **Surface colour is whitish / cream** (optionally with a faint pink or grey tinge), consistent with the myelinated white-matter mantle — not an arbitrary bright hue.
- [ ] **(If modelled) meninges and CSF space** end below the conus around the **S2** level (lumbar cistern), consistent with the cauda equina floating in CSF.

---

5. Kenhub — *Meninges* (dura, arachnoid, pia; epidural and subarachnoid spaces; denticulate ligaments). https://www.kenhub.com/en/library/anatomy/the-meninges
6. Wikipedia — *White matter* / *Grey matter* (colour of myelinated tracts vs. cell-body regions). https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/White_matter
